سائنسدان.e: Partial calque of English scientist, from سائنس (sāins, “science”) + دان (dān)

ex.u: سائنسی تحقیق
ex.e: scientific research

ex.u: مِرزا چَچا کے سایَہ میں پَروَرِش پاتے تھے۔ مَگَر اِتِّفاق یہ کِہ مَرْگِ ناگَہانی میں وہ مَر گَئے۔

سایہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَایَه (sāya). Doublet of چھاؤں (chāõ).

ex.u: سائِیں بابا
ex.e: Sai Baba

سائیں.e: Inherited from Apabhramsa साहि (sāhi), from Prakrit सामि (sāmi), from Sanskrit स्वामिन् (svāmin). Doublet of سَیّاں (saiyā̃). Sense 3 is from Sindhi سائِين (sāī̃).

senses.g: alternative form of سایَہ دار (sāya dār)

سب.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀩𑁆𑀩 (sabba), 𑀲𑀯𑁆𑀯 (savva), from Ashokan Prakrit 𑀲𑀯𑁆𑀯 (savva), 𑀲𑀭𑁆𑀯 (sarva), from Sanskrit सर्व (sárva), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *sárwas, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *sárwas, from Proto-Indo-European *solh₂-wó-s, from *solh₂- (“whole”). Doublet of ہر. Cognate with Kamkata-viri suv, Persian هر (har).

Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀩𑁆𑀩 (sabba), 𑀲𑀯𑁆𑀯 (savva), from Ashokan Prakrit 𑀲𑀯𑁆𑀯 (savva), 𑀲𑀭𑁆𑀯 (sarva), from Sanskrit सर्व (sárva), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *sárwas, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *sárwas, from Proto-Indo-European *solh₂-wó-s, from *solh₂- (“whole”). Doublet of ہر. Cognate with Kamkata-viri suv, Persian هر (har).

Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀩𑁆𑀩 (sabba), 𑀲𑀯𑁆𑀯 (savva), from Ashokan Prakrit 𑀲𑀯𑁆𑀯 (savva), 𑀲𑀭𑁆𑀯 (sarva), from Sanskrit सर्व (sárva), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *sárwas, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *sárwas, from Proto-Indo-European *solh₂-wó-s, from *solh₂- (“whole”). Doublet of ہر. Cognate with Kamkata-viri suv, Persian هر (har).

ex.u: صحارا دنیا کا سب سے بڑا ریگستان ہے۔
ex.r: sahārā duniyā kā sab se baṛā registān hai.
ex.e: The Sahara is the largest desert in the world.

سبب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَبَب (sabab), from Arabic سَبَب (sabab).

سبت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَبْت (sabt), from Arabic اَلسَّبْت (as-sabt).

senses.g: wow! - used sarcastically to express disapproval of something

سبحان اللہ.e: Unadapted borrowing from Arabic سُبْحَانَ اللّٰه (subḥāna llāh).

سبز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَبْز (sabz), itself from Middle Persian spz, sbz (sabz, “green, fresh”). Compare Kashmiri سَبٕز (sabụz).

ex.u: زِندَگی کا سَبَق
ex.e: life lesson

سبق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سبق (sabaq), from Arabic سَبَق (sabaq).

senses.g: Sibi (a city in Sibi district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

سبی.e: Named after Rani Sewi, a Hindu woman who ruled Balochistan before the 7th century.

senses.g: [one juz of or wholly] thirty ajza of the Qur'an – the Qur'an.

سپارہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سپاره (sipāra), a compound of سی (sī, “thirty”) + پاره (pāra, “piece; part; portion”). First attested as Middle Hindi سپارہ (sipāra) in 1421 A.D.

senses.g: sepoy; a Hindustani soldier (ie. a native soldier as opposed to an English soldier)

سپاہی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سِپَاهِی (sipāhī). First attested in c. 1635 Middle Hindi سپاہی (sipāhī). By surface analysis, سِپاہ (sipāh, “army”) + ی (ī).

سپتاہ.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit सप्ताह (saptāha). Doublet of ہَفْتَہ (hafta).

ex.u: تھوڑی دیر کے بعد، اُن کے سپنے سچّے نکلے۔
ex.r: thoṛī der ke ba'ad, un ke sapne sacce nikle.
ex.e: After a little while, their dreams became true.

ex.u: ناخدا بننا ہمیشہ اس کا سپنا تھا۔
ex.r: nāxudā bannā hameśa us kā sapnā thā.
ex.e: It was always his dream to become a pilot.

سپنا.e: Cognates include Latin somnus, Ancient Greek ὕπνος (húpnos), Old English swefn, and (English sweven).

senses.g: A salutation; a greeting used for most purposes including hello, good morning/afternoon, and goodbye
gold or silver pieces that are added to traditional Pakistani clothing to make them shine
a white mark on a horse's head
shiny pieces attached to a necklace

ستارہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سِتارَه (sitāra), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *Hstar-, from Proto-Indo-European *h₂stḗr. Doublet of تارا (tārā).

ـستان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ـِسْتَان (-istān), from Middle Persian -stʾn' (-estān), from Old Persian 𐎿𐎫𐎠𐎴 (s-t-a-n /⁠stāna⁠/), from Proto-Iranian *stā́nam, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *stʰáHnam, from Proto-Indo-European *steh₂- (“to stand”). Doublet of تھانَہ (thāna) and سْتھان (sthān).

ستائیس.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀸𑀯𑀻𑀲 (sattāvīsa), from Sanskrit सप्तविंशति (saptaviṃśati).

سترہ.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀲𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀭𑀲 (sattarasa), from Sanskrit सप्तदश (saptádaśa).

ستمبر.e: Borrowed from Portuguese setembro, influenced by English September, ultimately from Latin September. Compare Konkani सेतेंब्र (setembra), Marathi सेप्तेंबर (septembar), सप्तेंबर (saptembar).

senses.g: Praise, eulogy, a hymn of praise, ode etc.

ex.u: اس کا گفٹ ستھان میں ہے۔
ex.r: us kā gifṭ sthān mẽ hai.
ex.e: His gift is at a location.

ex.u: یہاں تمہرے لیئے ستھان نیہں ہے۔
ex.r: yahā̃ tumhe līe sthān nahī̃ hai.
ex.e: There is no room for you here.

ستھان.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit स्थान (sthāna). Compare with Avestan 𐬯𐬙𐬁𐬥𐬀- (stāna-) and Persian ـستان (-estân). Doublet of ـستان (-(i)stān), تھان (thān), and تھانَہ (thāna).

senses.g: sujud, a single act of prostration
the name of the 32nd chapter of the Qur'an

سجدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَجْدَه (sajda), from Arabic سَجْدَة (sajda).

سچ.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi سچ (sac, sacc /⁠sc⁠/) (c. 1635), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀘𑁆𑀘 (sacca), from Sanskrit सत्य (satyá, “promise”).

سچا.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi سچا (sca /⁠saccā⁠/) (c. 1564), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀘𑁆𑀘 (sacca), from Sanskrit सत्य (sátya).

senses.g: inflection of سَچّا (saccā): — direct masculine plural
inflection of سَچّا (saccā): — oblique masculine singular/plural

سحر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَحَر (sahar), from Arabic سَحَر (saḥar).

senses.g: a female given name, Sehar, from Arabic

سحر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَحَر (sahar), from Arabic سَحَر (saḥar).
Borrowed from Classical Persian سِحْر (sihr), from Arabic سِحْر (siḥr).

senses.g: suhur (a pre-dawn breakfast during the Ramadan fasting)

ex.u: یہ کئی کئی دِنوں کا ایک روزہ ہوتا اِفطار اور سحری کے موقع پر آپؐ کچھ نہ کھاتے
ex.e: It used to be a fast of several days, and he [Muhammad P.B.U.H] did not eat anything when it came to Iftar and Suhoor

سحری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَحَرِی (saharī). By surface analysis, سَحَر (sahar, “dawn”) + ـِی (-ī).
Borrowed from Classical Persian سَحَرِی (saharī). By surface analysis, سَحَر (sahar, “dawn”) + ـِی (-ī).

سخت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سخت (saxt), from Middle Persian 𐭮𐭠𐭤𐭨 (saxt), 𐭮𐭤𐭨 (saxt).

سخی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سخی (saxī), from Arabic سَخِيّ (saḵiyy).
Borrowed from Classical Persian سخی (saxī), from Arabic سَخِيّ (saḵiyy).

senses.g: night and day, to end of chapter

سدابہار.e: From Sanskrit सदा (sadā, “always, ever”) + Classical Persian بهار (bahār, “spring season”).

ex.u: سدن میں موجود ہر ایک شخص کو خوش آمدید
ex.e: Welcome to every member presented in the chamber.

سدن.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit सदन (sadana), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *sed-.

ex.u: پیغمبر محمد معاشرے کے ایک سُدھارک تھے.
ex.e: Prophet Muhammad happened to be a social reformer.

سدھارنا.e: Derived from سدھار (sudhār), which is from Sanskrit *शुद्धकार (*śuddhakāra, “the act of purifying or removing errors”), from शुद्ध (śuddha) + कार (kāra) (compare Sanskrit शुद्धीकरोति (śuddhīkaroti), from शुद्धीकृ (śuddhīkṛ, “to purify, to remove errors”, root), from शुद्ध (śuddha, adjective) + ईकृ (īkṛ, root form)). Cognate with Punjabi ਸੁਧਾਰਨਾ (sudhārnā) / سُدھارنا (sudhārnā), Nepali सुधार्नु (sudhārnu), Bengali শুধরানো (sudhranô), Odia ସୁଧାରିବା (sudhāribā), Gujarati સુધારવું (sudhārvũ), Marathi सुधारणे (sudhārṇe), Assamese শুধৰোৱা (xudhorüa).

سر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سر (sar). Cognate with Sanskrit शिरस् (śiras). Doublet of سر (sir, “head”).
Borrowed from Classical Persian سر (sir(r)), from Arabic سِرّ (sirr).
Borrowed from Classical Persian سر (sur), from Sanskrit शूल (śūla).

ex.u: سَرَاسَر جُھوٹھ
ex.e: completely false

سراسر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَرَاسَر (sarāsar). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi سراسر (srasr /⁠sarāsar⁠/).

senses.g: Saranan (a city in Pishin district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

سراہنا.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi [Term?] (c. 1611), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀮𑀸𑀳𑀤𑀺 (salāhadi), from Sanskrit श्लाघते (ślā́ghate).

سرائے.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سرای (sarāy), from Middle Persian slʾd (srāy).

senses.g: Serbia (a country on the Balkan Peninsula in Southeast Europe)
extent (ie. extent or corner of something)

سرحد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَرْحَد (sarhad, “border”). First attested in c. 1657 as Middle Hindi سرحد (srḥd /⁠sarhad⁠/).. By surface analysis, سَر (sar, “head”) + حَد (had, “boundary”).

سرخ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سرخ (surx). Doublet of شکر (śukra, “bright”).

سرخی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سرخی. By surface analysis, سرخ + ـی (-ī).

سرد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سرد (sard), from Middle Persian slt' (sard).

سردی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَرْدی (sardē). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi سردی (srdy /⁠sardī⁠/). By surface analysis, سَرْد (sard) + ی (ī).

سرسائی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀭𑀲𑁆𑀲𑀤𑀻 (sarassadī), from Sanskrit सरस्वती (sarasvatī, “Sarasvati”).

senses.g: Saraswati, the goddess of knowledge and speech
the Saraswati river. Arguably, of which only a small stream remains as the Ghaggar River
a female given name, Sarasvati, from Sanskrit, of Hindu usage

سرسوتی.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit सरस्वती (sarasvatī). Doublet of سرسائی (sarsā'ī).

ex.u: سَرْسوں کَا تیل
ex.e: mustard oil

سرسوں.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi سرسوں (srsvṉ /⁠sarsõ⁠/) (c. 1751), from Old Hindi سرسوں (srsvṉ /⁠sarsõ⁠/) (c. 1300s), from Prakrit 𑀲𑀭𑀺𑀲𑀯 (sarisava), from Sanskrit सर्षप (sarṣapa). Cognates Cognate with Assamese সবিয়হ (xobih), Bengali সরিষা (śoriśa), Gujarati સરસવ (sarsav), Marathi शिरीस (śirīs), and Punjabi سرہوں / Punjabi ਸਰ੍ਹੋਂ (sarhõ).

ex.u: آواز اونچی کر کے بولنا میری سرشت میں نہیں
ex.r: āvāz ū̃ci kar ke bolnā merī sariśt mẽ nahī̃
ex.e: Speaking in a loud voice is not in my nature

senses.g: a female given name, Shristi, from Sanskrit

سرشٹی.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit सृष्टि (sṛṣṭi). Doublet of سِٹّھِی (siṭṭhī).
Learned borrowing from Sanskrit सृष्टि (sṛṣṭi). Doublet of سِٹّھِی (siṭṭhī).

senses.g: ready to sacrifice one's life; extremely loyal; true blue

سرفروش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سرفروش (sarfarōš), from سر (sar, “head”) + فروش (-farôš, “to sell, to trade (something) off”).

ex.u: تَلَن٘گانَہ سَرْکار
ex.e: Government of Telangana.

سرگ.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit स्वर्ग (svargá). Compare Punjabi سورگ / ਸੁਰਗ (surg). Doublet of سورگ (svarg).

senses.g: Sargodha (a city in Sargodha district, Punjab, Pakistan)

سرگودھا.e: From Punjabi سرگودھا (sargodhā), from ਸਰ (sar, “pond, pool”) + ਗੋਧਾ (godhā, “a Hindu faqir”).

senses.g: the most important possessions of one's life, the most vital belongings of one's life

ex.u: میرے بچے اور میرے شوہر میرے سرمایہ ہیں
ex.r: mere bacce aur mere śauhar mere sarmāya ha͠i
ex.e: my children and my husband are the most important possessions of my life

senses.g: money-idolatry, the worship and idolization of money and wealth

سرمایہ پرستی.e: From سرمایہ (sarmāya, “capital; money; wealth”) + پرستی (parastī, “worship; adoration”).

senses.g: capitalism, an economic system wherein capital is accumulated from labor by a capitalist

سرمہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سرمه (surma), from Turkic sürmek ("to draw along, rub, smear") or, alternatively, from Proto-Indo-European *ḱel- (“to cover, conceal”).

senses.g: tunnel — an underground passage (often used to trespass)
tunnel — a passage through an obstacle

سرنگ.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi سرنگ (srng) (c. 1678), from Prakrit 𑀲𑀼𑀭𑀼𑀁𑀕𑀸 (suruṃgā), from Sanskrit सुरङ्गा (suraṅgā) or सुरुङ्गा (suruṅgā), ultimately from Ancient Greek σῦρῐγξ (sûrĭnx). Doublet of سِرِن٘ج (siriñj). Cognates Compare with Odia ସୁରଙ୍ଗ (suraṅga), Bengali সুড়ঙ্গ (śuṛoṅgo), Assamese সুৰুঙা (xuruṅa) and Punjabi سُرَن٘گ (suraṉg) / ਸੁਰੰਗ (suraṅg).

سرور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سرور (surūr), from Arabic سُرُور (surūr).

senses.g: a heavenly voice which comes from the divine, an unseen voice from Heaven; the voice of an angel.

سرہانہ.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *शिर आधान (*śira-ādhāna). Compare Punjabi سِرْہاݨا (sirhāṇā) / Punjabi ਸਿਰਹਾਣਾ (sirhāṇā).

senses.g: Sri Lanka (a country in South Asia)

سری لنکا.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit श्री लङ्का (śrī laṅkā, literally “holy island”).

senses.g: Srinagar (a city and district, the summer capital of Jammu and Kashmir, India)

ex.u: سرینگر میں دریائے جہلم کے کنارے ویران پڑے ہیں، کچھ باقی شہر کی طرح۔
ex.r: sirī nagar meṉ daryā'e jihlam ke kināre vīrān paṛe haiṉ, kuch bāqī śahar kī t̤araḥ.
ex.e: In Srinagar, the banks of river Jhelum remain vacant, somewhat like the rest of the city.

سری نگر.e: From Sanskrit श्रीनगर (śrīnagara), from श्री (śrī) + नगर (nagara, “town, city”). Compare Kashmiri سِریٖنَگَر (sirīnagar).

سریر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سریر (sarīr), from Arabic سَرِير (sarīr).

senses.g: alternative form of سِری نَگَر (sirī nagar)

سڑک.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi سڙک (sṛk /⁠saṛak⁠/) (c. 1611), from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀲𑀟𑀓𑁆𑀓 (*saḍakka), from Sanskrit सृ॒ति (sṛtí) extended with Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓𑁆𑀓- (-kka-).

ex.u: یہ گاڑی بہت سست ہے۔
ex.e: This car is very slow

سستا.e: Unknown. Compare Punjabi ਸਸਤਾ (sastā), Nepali सस्तो (sasto), Gujarati સસ્તું (sastũ), Sindhi سَستو (sasto), Bengali সস্তা (śosta), Odia ଶସ୍ତା (śastā), Assamese সস্তা (xosta), Sylheti ꠢꠍꠔꠣ (hosta), Marathi स्वस्त (svasta, hypercorrection of सस्त (sasta)).

سستی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سستی (sustī). سُسْت (sust, “lazy, idle”) + ـی (-ī).
See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

سسر.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀼𑀲𑀼𑀭 (susura), from Sanskrit श्वशुर (śvaśura), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *swáśuras, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *swáćuras, from Proto-Indo-European *swéḱuros. Doublet of خُسَر (xusar). Cognate with Punjabi سَوہرا (sauhrā), Saraiki سَوہرا (sauhrā), Sindhi سَهُرو (sahuro), Burushaski [script needed] (šūšur), Kalasha [script needed] (išpášur), Khowar اِشپاشُور (išpašúr), Torwali شأژو (šλẓo) and far cognate with Pashto سخر (/⁠sxr⁠/).
Inherited from Sanskrit शिशिर (śiśira), from Proto-Indo-European *ḱel-.

senses.g: the winter season of Magh and Phagan.

سسر.e: Inherited from Sanskrit शिशिर (śiśira), from Proto-Indo-European *ḱel-.

سسرال.e: Inherited from Sanskrit श्वशुरघर (śvaśuraghara), compound of श्वशुर (śvaśura, “father-in-law”) + घर (ghara, “house”), literally “father-in-law's house”.

ex.u: کاٹو، سَسُری
ex.e: Piss off, bitch

سسری.e: Feminine of سَسُر (sasur, “father-in-law”), inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀼𑀲𑀼𑀭 (susura), from Sanskrit श्वशुर (śváśura).

سطح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سطح (sath), from Arabic سَطْح (saṭḥ).

سطحی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَطْحِی (sathī), from Arabic سَطْحِيّ (saṭḥiyy).

سطر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَطْر (satr), from Arabic سَطْر (saṭr).

senses.g: a device used for sexual penetration; dildo

سعتر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سعتر (sa'tar), from Arabic سَعْتَر (saʕtar), alternative form of زَعْتَر (zaʕtar). Sense 2 originates from the historical use of marjoram and similar plants as dildoes in early modern India, particularly during the Mughal era.

senses.g: Saudi Arabia (a country in the Middle East)

ex.u: مَیں نے اَپنے دوسْت کِی سِفارِش پَر یہ کِتَاب پَڑِھی۔
ex.r: ma͠i ne apne dost kī sifāriś par ye kitāb paṛhī.
ex.e: I read this book upon my friend's recommendation.

سفر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سفر (safar), from Arabic سَفَر (safar). Cognate of English safari.

senses.g: oblique plural of سَفَر نامَہ (safar nāma)

سفر نامہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سفر نامه (safar-nāma).

senses.g: inflection of سَفَر نامَہ (safar nāma): — direct plural
inflection of سَفَر نامَہ (safar nāma): — oblique/vocative singular

سفید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَفید (safēd), from earlier Early New Persian سَپید (sapēd), from Middle Persian spyt', from Proto-Iranian *cwaytáh, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *ćwaytás, from Proto-Indo-European *ḱweyt-.

سفیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سفیر (safīr), from Arabic سَفِير (safīr).

senses.g: Scythian; a warlike nomadic people from Scythia
the Indo-Scythians of ancient Afghanistan and Pakistan

سکا.e: Either from Classical Persian سکا (sakā) or a modern learned borrowing from an ancient Indo-Iranian language referring to the same nomadic peoples.

سکنا.e: Inherited from Apabhramsa सक्कइ (sakkaï), from Prakrit 𑀲𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀇 (sakkaï), from Sanskrit श॒क्नोति॑ (śaknóti), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *ćaknáwti, from Proto-Indo-European *ḱek- + *-néwti.

ex.u: تلوار اور ایمان سے فتح حاصل کر کے وہ ہر گاؤں اور شہر، ہر پہاڑ اور ہر میدان کا سکندر بن گیا۔
ex.r: talvār aur īmān se fatah hasil kar ke vo har gāoñ aur šehár, har pahāṛ aur har maydān kā sikandar ban geyā
ex.e: with sword and faith, yielding victory, he became the sovereign of every village and city, every mountain and every plain.

سکندر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سِکَنْدَر (sikandar, “Alexander The Great”), from Middle Persian swkndl (sikandar), ultimately from Ancient Greek Ἀλέξανδρος (Aléxandros). First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi سکندر (skndr /⁠sikandar⁠/).

senses.g: Alexander the Great; king of Macedon whose undefeated campaign spanned across much of West Asia, Egypt and into the Indian subcontinent, establishing one of the largest empires in classical antiquity.
a male given name, Sikandar, from Ancient Greek, equivalent to English Alexander

سکندر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سِکَنْدَر (sikandar, “Alexander The Great”), from Middle Persian swkndl (sikandar), ultimately from Ancient Greek Ἀλέξανδρος (Aléxandros). First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi سکندر (skndr /⁠sikandar⁠/).

ex.u: Near-synonym: وِرام (virām)

سکون.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سُکُون (sukūn), from Arabic سُكُون (sukūn).

senses.g: a certain currency used in the Mughal Empire, sicca

سکہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سِکَّه (sikka), from Arabic سِكَّة (sikka).

سکھ.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀲𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀔 (sukkha), from Sanskrit सुख (sukha).

senses.g: Sikh, a follower of Sikhism (سکھ مت)

سکھانا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀲𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀔𑀸𑀯𑁂𑀇 (sukkhāvei), from Sanskrit शुष्कति (śuṣkati)।

senses.g: Sukkur (a city in Sukkur district, Sindh, Pakistan)

سکھی.e: First attested in c. 1625 as Middle Hindi سکھی (sakhī), borrowed from Sanskrit सखी (sakhī).

سگ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سگ (sag, “dog”). Cognate with Kashmiri ہوٗن (hūn), Kalasha شوںہ (šọ̃́a) and Shina شُن (śun)۔

سلاح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سِلَاح (silāh), borrowed from Arabic سِلَاح (silāḥ).

سلاد.e: Borrowed from Portuguese salada or English salad.

senses.g: interjection used to greet or often fare adieu to someone.

ex.u:
سَلام! ― salām! ― hello!
سَلام عَلَیکُم! ― salām 'alaikum! ― hello!

سلام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَلَام (salām), itself from Arabic سَلَام (salām). First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi سلام (slam).

senses.g: a salute (chiefly military but not limited to).

ex.u: پاکِسْتان تُجھے سَلام
ex.e: Salute to you, O Pakistan

ex.u: اَبُّو کو میرا سَلام دینا
ex.e: Send my regards to your father.

senses.g: one of the attributes of Allah

سلام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَلَام (salām), itself from Arabic سَلَام (salām). First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi سلام (slam).

senses.g: a hi, hello (term for a mere brief exchange of pleasantries as opposed to a longer discussion with a person)

سلام علیکم.e: From the Arabic سَلَامٌ عَلَيْكُم (salāmun ʕalaykum, “peace be upon you”).

سلامت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَلَامَت (salāmat), from Arabic سَلَامَة (salāma).

senses.g: bless you, stay safe and healthy
bless you, stay safe and healthy
Seljuk (of or related to Seljuk, his dynasty, their empire, or their period of rule)

سلجوقی.e: Borrowed from Arabic سَلْجُوقِيّ (saljūqiyy). By surface analysis, سلجوق (saljūq) + ـی (-ī, nisba suffix).

senses.g: Seljuk (a member of the Seljuk dynasty, a person of their empire)

سلجوقی.e: Borrowed from Arabic سَلْجُوقِيّ (saljūqiyy). By surface analysis, سلجوق (saljūq) + ـی (-ī, nisba suffix).

senses.g: line, sequence, chain (narrow and blended meaning)

سلسلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سلسله (silsila), from Arabic سِلْسِلَة (silsila). First attested in 1707 A.D.

سلطان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سلطان (sultān), from Arabic سُلْطَان (sulṭān).

senses.g: Sultan Bazar (a neighbourhood of Hyderabad, Telangana, India)

سلطان بازار.e: Named after Nawab Sultanuddin Khan, from سُلْطان (sultān) + بازار (bāzār).

senses.g: kingdom, empire, dominion, realm, reign, Reich

سلطنت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سلطنت (saltanat), from Arabic سَلْطَنَة (salṭana).

senses.g: alternative form of شلوار قمیض (śalvār qamīz, “shalwar kameez”)

سلوک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سُلُوک (sulūk), from Arabic سُلُوك (sulūk). Compare Punjabi سَلُوک (salūk) / ਸਲੂਕ (salūk) and Gujarati સલૂક (salūk).

ex.u:
Ikram Basra, Koi Tagore Ki Kawita Hai:
تجھ کو سلہٹ میں سوچ کر لکھا
tujh ko silhaṭ mein soch kar likkhā
I wrote to you thoughtfully in Sylhet.

سلہٹ.e: Borrowed from Bengali শিলহট (śilohoṭ), archaic form of সিলেট (śileṭ), ultimately from Sanskrit শ্রীহট্ট (śrīhaṭṭa).

senses.g: a male given name, Suleman or Sulaiman, from Arabic

سلیمان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سلیمان (soleymân), from Arabic سُلَيْمَان (sulaymān).

senses.g: Sulaymaniyah (a city, the capital of Sulaymaniyah Governorate in the Kurdistan Region, Iraq)
There is no fully established single-word equivalent of slang in Urdu. The English word slang is now commonly used in Urdu as well.

ex.u: آرْیَہ سَماج
ex.e: Arya Samaj

سماچار.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit समाचार (samācāra, “proceeding, conduct, news, information”).

سماچار پتر.e: Possibly a calque of English newspaper.

ex.u: یہ ہی اس مسئلہ کا سَمادھان ہے.
ex.e: This is the solution of this problem.

سماں.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi سماں (smaṉ) (c. 1780), from Prakrit 𑀲𑀫𑀅 (samaa), from Sanskrit स॒म॒य (samayá). Doublet of سَمَے (samai).

senses.g: of or relating to the sky; celestial, heavenly
Sambrial (a city in Sialkot district, Punjab, Pakistan)

سمجھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀁𑀩𑀼𑀚𑁆𑀛 (saṃbujjha). Compare also سمجھنا (samajhnā).

سمجھنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀁𑀩𑀼𑀚𑁆𑀛𑀤𑀺 (saṃbujjhadi), from Sanskrit सम्बुध्यते (sambudhyate). Cognate with Punjabi ਸਮਝਣਾ (samjhaṇā) / سَمجھݨا (samjhṇā), Gujarati સમજવું (samajvũ), Marathi समजणे (samajṇe).

سمدر.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit समुद्र (samudra). Doublet of سمندر (samandar).

ex.u: اورنگزیب ہندوستان کے ایک عظیم سمراٹ تھے
ex.e: Aurangzeb happened to be mighty emperor of Hindoostan

سمراٹ.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit सम्राट् (samrāṭ), nominative singular of सम्राज् (samrāj).

senses.g: the body of law as delivered originally by tradition

ex.u: وہ بہت ساری سیاسی پارٹیوں کا سَمَرْتھَک ہے.
ex.e: He is a supporter of many political parties.

سمسم.e: Borrowed from Arabic سِمْسِم (simsim), borrowed from Aramaic שׁוּמְשֻׁומָא (šumššumā), borrowed from Akkadian 𒊭𒈦𒌑𒈬 (šamaššammum), from šaman + šammum.
Borrowed from Arabic سِمْسِم (simsim), borrowed from Aramaic שׁוּמְשֻׁומָא (šumššumā), borrowed from Akkadian 𒊭𒈦𒌑𒈬 (šamaššammum), from šaman + šammum.

سمع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَمْع (sam'), from Arabic سَمْع (samʕ).

سمعی بصری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَمْعِی بَصَرِی (sam'ī basarī), from Arabic سَمْعِي بَصَرِي (samʕī baṣarī).

ex.u: سمکالین سائنس کی ترقی بہت عجیب ہے
ex.e: The development of contemporary science is wonderful.

سمکھ.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit सम्मुख (sam-mukhá). Doublet of سَامْنَا (sāmnā). Cognate with Marathi सम्मुख (sammukh).
Learned borrowing from Sanskrit सम्मुख (sam-mukhá). Doublet of سَامْنَا (sāmnā). Cognate with Marathi सम्मुख (sammukh).
